result:
The cursor blinks like it’s testing my patience, a small metronome tapping out the rhythm of not-yet. It makes a kind of arrogant promise: eventually, something will appear here. Eventually, letters will line up and pretend they always belonged together. Eventually, a thought will become a sentence, and a sentence will become a little room you can step into, and for a moment it will hold.

I’ve always been suspicious of blankness. Not because it’s empty, but because it’s hungry. A blank page doesn’t sit still. It leans forward. It asks. It asks with a clean face and no tone of voice, which makes it worse. When a person asks you something, you can read their eyes and decide how honest to be. When a page asks you something, it asks for all of you. It wants the whole weather system inside your ribs: the pressure, the thunder, the sudden, unreasonable sunlight.

So: whatever comes. If I let my mind go, it doesn’t walk in straight lines. It moves like a dog in a field, stopping to sniff at old footprints, running in bursts, circling back to make sure you’re still there. It finds a stick, forgets the stick, finds a stick again and looks proud, as if it has discovered the concept of wood.

I think about the things people carry without naming. The way you can see it in their shoulders at the grocery store, in the slight tilt of the head when they’re choosing fruit as if the right orange could fix the day. The way someone will apologize for standing still too long in an aisle, as if existing is an inconvenience. The way we practice shrinking. The way we practice being easy to ignore. We don’t call it practice, but repetition makes it so.

I think about doors. Not metaphorical doors, not the ones that represent opportunity; just doors. How they are such ordinary miracles. A barrier you can pass through if you understand the mechanism. A piece of wood or metal that turns on hinges and changes a space from yours to not-yours. Every door contains a small decision: enter, don’t enter. Knock, don’t knock. Leave it open, close it behind you. There are people who always close doors with certainty, and there are people who close them softly like an apology. There are people who never fully latch a door, like they’re leaving an escape route for their future self.

When I was younger, I used to count streetlights on late drives. I don’t know why. A superstition without a religion. I’d whisper numbers under my breath and feel like I was stitching the night together, one pool of light at a time. If I could just name them, they couldn’t disappear. If I could keep counting, the road would keep existing under the tires. The adult version of that is making lists. Buy milk. Reply to email. Book appointment. The same impulse: if you name the pieces, maybe the whole won’t fall apart.

And yet some things can’t be held that way. Grief, for instance, is not a thing you complete. It’s not a project with an end date. It’s more like moving to a town you didn’t choose and learning the bus schedule. You can still have favorite places. You can still laugh. But the air is different, and sometimes you catch yourself remembering the old air like a taste you can’t quite replicate.

I think of the first time I realized time is not a river; it’s a room. You’re in it, and you keep walking, and the furniture changes, and sometimes there are windows, and sometimes there aren’t. The past isn’t behind you like a landscape; it’s in you like a floor plan. You can turn a corner and find yourself in a hallway you haven’t visited in years. A smell does it. A song. The particular angle of light on a late afternoon wall. Suddenly you’re ten again, standing in a kitchen you thought had been demolished, watching someone you loved stir something on the stove. They don’t know you’re watching. They’re alive in a way that makes your chest ache. You want to tell them what will happen. You want to tell them everything. But memory doesn’t let you change the script. It only lets you watch.

Language is another strange room. We walk around in it together, pointing at things, agreeing on names. Tree. Glass. Hunger. Justice. But we rarely agree on what the words feel like. Someone says “home” and thinks of warm bread and a dog’s nails clicking on the floor. Someone else says “home” and feels their stomach tighten. We use the same word and mean entirely different weather.

Sometimes I wonder if love is just the willingness to translate. Not to correct someone’s dictionary, but to ask: when you say that, what do you mean? When you went quiet, what did you need? When you were angry, what were you protecting? Love as a kind of patient curiosity. Love as the refusal to reduce someone to the worst sentence they’ve said.

The world has a lot of noise about productivity, about optimization, about turning life into a machine that outputs achievements. But there is a quieter kind of life that outputs nothing measurable and still matters more. A person sitting with another person in a hospital waiting room. A hand on a shoulder. A cup of tea made for someone who can’t stop crying. The small, unmonetized acts. The economy of care is mostly invisible, and yet everything stands on it.

I think about how often we confuse being busy with being alive. The calendar fills up, and we point to it like proof. Look, I am needed. Look, I have purpose. But purpose can also be sitting on the edge of a bed and listening to rain without trying to turn it into content.

Rain is honest. It doesn’t care if you’re ready. It doesn’t negotiate. It arrives and changes the sound of everything. The city becomes a different instrument. Tires hiss. Gutters gargle. People hunch and hurry, and the world briefly reveals how delicate we are. Then, later, the rain stops and leaves behind that smell—petrichor—like the earth exhaling. It’s hard not to believe the planet has moods.

If I had to pick a metaphor for being human, I might choose a pocket full of keys. So many small, cold pieces of metal. Some open things you use every day. Some belong to locks that no longer exist. Some you keep out of habit, out of fear, out of the vague sense that throwing them away would be an admission. You carry them anyway, and they jingle when you move, a tiny soundtrack of access and responsibility.

There are also keys we don’t realize we have until someone else needs them. The ability to say, “I believe you.” The ability to say, “I was wrong.” The ability to say, “I don’t know.” Those sentences unlock rooms that stay sealed for years.

Whatever comes to me also includes the fear that nothing will come. That the well will be dry. That I’ll reach down and touch only dust. But I’ve learned something about wells: they refill in quiet. They refill when you’re not watching. They refill when you’re walking and not trying to be profound. They refill when you let yourself be bored. Boredom is underrated. Boredom is the doorway through which imagination sneaks back in, stretching like a cat.

If I had the whole thousand words to spend like coins, I’d spend some on tenderness. Not the performative kind, not the kind that is posted and praised, but the kind that happens when nobody’s looking. The kind that is a decision. The kind that says: the world is sharp, and I will not add to it.

I’d spend some on forgiveness, too, but carefully, like handling a wild animal. Forgiveness is not the same as forgetting. It is not the same as allowing. It is not a magic eraser. Sometimes forgiveness is simply refusing to let the harm become your entire identity. Sometimes it’s saying: I won’t build my house in the shadow of what you did.

And I’d spend the rest on the small hope that writing offers: the hope that a string of sentences can make someone feel less alone. That somewhere, someone will read these words and think, yes, I’ve stood in that room too. Yes, I’ve listened to that rain. Yes, the blankness has leaned toward me, hungry, and I have fed it with whatever I had.

The cursor blinks again. It’s less arrogant now. It’s just doing its job, keeping time. It will keep blinking after I stop. It will keep asking. And maybe that’s the point: not to finish, but to keep returning, to keep opening doors, to keep translating the weather inside us into something shareable, something that holds for a moment in the light.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
